title: Ranked frontier selection is required for representative portal crawls
summary: First-seen queue insertion caused portal homepages to expand into help and utility paths before representative subservices. Structured link candidates plus scoring and diversity caps corrected this on Naver and generalized to other sites.
nextAction: Add more fixtures to suppress account-route and utility API links that still rank too high on some portal homepages.

title: Multi-host recursive crawls need host-aware saved paths
summary: Recursive registrable-domain crawls can save different host root pages to the same HTML path unless host prefixes are preserved. This was the main structural reason Naver replay fidelity looked worse than the crawl itself.
nextAction: Keep host-aware path resolution aligned across save, rewrite, replay serving, and verification code paths.

title: Replay comparisons need charset-aware decoded text
summary: Portal pages and help pages can appear broken in title and text drift metrics when capture relies on naive UTF-8 assumptions. Preserving charset hints and decoded text alongside raw bodies reduces false title drift and improves replay diagnosis.
nextAction: Expand charset-aware extraction into more manifest summaries and fixture coverage for non-UTF pages.

title: Most requestfailed logs on portal sites are noise rather than fatal crawl breakage
summary: Naver produced large volumes of `ERR_ABORTED` requests from adtech, telemetry, lazy thumbnails, and dynamic widget chunks. The main page still replayed successfully, which means raw requestfailed volume alone is not a fidelity metric.
nextAction: Classify failed requests before surfacing them directly so users can distinguish noise from replay-critical failures.

title: Malformed third-party CSS should fail soft instead of aborting the clone
summary: A malformed CSS payload with an unclosed bracket from a Naver-related asset was enough to stop the full clone until CSS processing gained a regex fallback for imports and asset URLs.
nextAction: Add more malformed CSS fixtures so regex fallback remains bounded and predictable.

title: Auto-scroll and screenshot height checks must not depend on document.body existing
summary: A narrow Netflix crawl showed that some pages can lack a usable document.body during scroll and screenshot preparation, which caused `scrollHeight` null crashes. Shared scroll-root measurement with fail-soft warnings keeps the crawl moving while still capturing lazy-load opportunities when the document can scroll normally.
nextAction: Watch for non-body scroll containers and add a bounded generic fallback only if future live sites still miss lazy-loaded content after the body-null fix.

title: CSS-linked first-paint assets need a late-binding rewrite pass
summary: Netflix replay still leaked render-critical font requests even after the font files existed locally because an early CSS pass kept absolute CDN URLs. A final CSS rewrite pass plus shared asset registry registration fixed this and reduced replay verification external requests for render-critical assets to zero.
nextAction: Keep checking for other CSS files that are rewritten before their owned assets are captured, and prefer finalization passes over site-specific font hacks.

title: Cache-backed state refresh queries should not always be strict replay blockers
summary: Netflix public landing pages embed membership fallback state inline and use a cache-and-network GraphQL hook for MembershipStatus. Treating that query as strict render-critical created false replay warnings even though first paint succeeded without the request, so replay candidates now distinguish strict render-critical from informational supporting runtime.
nextAction: Keep reviewing same-site bootstrap endpoints so auxiliary support calls like consent or help telemetry do not remain in the strict replay-expected set without visible UI evidence.

title: Replay adapters should stay visible before service-worker interception becomes a default runtime layer
summary: Official service worker fetch and Cache API docs confirm that a registered worker can satisfy document and subresource requests before network-visible adapter routes are observed. For this repository, that means generated replay should keep service workers disabled by default until request visibility, cache invalidation, and mock attribution stay debuggable.
nextAction: Do not introduce generated replay service workers until the adapter can report whether a request was satisfied by a worker, an Express mock, or a static file without hiding route-level diagnostics.

title: Next.js App Router needs different replay heuristics than Pages Router bootstrap blobs
summary: Next.js documentation shows that Pages Router relies on inlined `__NEXT_DATA__`, while App Router leans on server component streaming, server-side fetches, and Data Cache behavior. Replay classification should therefore treat App Router pages as mixed bootstrap-plus-streaming surfaces instead of assuming one inline JSON blob captures first-paint state.
nextAction: Add framework-aware bootstrap detection so Pages Router pages prefer inline JSON preservation while App Router pages inspect streamed payload markers and deferred runtime fetches separately.

title: Persisted GraphQL queries can be matched without the full query text
summary: Apollo APQ documentation shows that clients may omit the GraphQL document and send only operation metadata plus a persisted query hash. Replay matching should therefore use operation name, variables shape or hash, and persisted query metadata before assuming a mock is unmatchable because `query` is absent.
nextAction: Extend GraphQL replay candidate keys and adapter fallback rules so APQ-style requests can match mocks deterministically without weakening pathname or method contracts.

title: Observed requests are not the same thing as first-paint requirements
summary: Next.js request memoization and Apollo cache-and-network behavior both show that a request can exist during live capture without being necessary for first paint during replay. Replay verification should keep using bootstrap evidence, visible-section dependence, and supporting-runtime buckets instead of equating every observed request with a strict replay dependency.
nextAction: Keep separating strict render-critical candidates from supporting refresh traffic, and add more live fixtures where first paint succeeds entirely from inline bootstrap data.

title: Payload semantics can override same-site POST replay heuristics
summary: Netflix public landing replay still showed a strict blocker until `help.netflix.com/cl2` was inspected as a `CompactConsolidatedLoggingEnvelope` payload. The path alone was ambiguous, but the body shape clearly represented client logging state, so replay classification now treats same-site POST logging envelopes as non-critical instead of replay bootstrap.
nextAction: Look for other same-site POST endpoints whose body shape identifies them as logging or telemetry transports before leaving them in the strict replay-expected set.

title: Serialized bootstrap URLs need the same local-first rewrite policy as rendered anchors
summary: Netflix public pages showed that footer and login navigation can still escape to live destinations even when visible `<a href>` values are rewritten locally, because the SPA runtime rehydrates from serialized config containing same-site URLs. Replay fidelity improves when inline same-site navigation URLs use exact URL mapping first and then a unique host+pathname fallback to captured HTML, while uncaptured same-site targets remain disabled instead of leaking live navigation.
nextAction: Reuse the exact-plus-unique-path fallback for other SPA bootstrap containers and keep validating that captured public targets stay local while uncaptured public targets degrade to disabled links instead of live navigation.

title: Charset fidelity depends on preserving full transport metadata instead of bare mime types
summary: Replay comparison quality degraded on legacy portal pages because capture was passing bare `mimeType` values into charset detection, which drops `Content-Type` charset evidence before HTML/CSS decoding decisions are made. Generic replay diagnostics improve when transport charset, BOM, and in-document declarations are preserved together and surfaced as encoding source, confidence, and mismatch signals in page-level reports.
nextAction: Keep page-level verifier output tied to encoding confidence so legacy portal text drift can be separated from real rendering gaps before widening strict runtime fixes.

title: Legacy portal CSS recovery needs canonicalization plus explicit failure summaries
summary: A fresh Jongno public rerun showed that generic CSS late-binding is not enough on legacy portal sites unless the processor also preserves already-localized relative paths, canonicalizes malformed same-origin asset URLs, and records unresolved CSS-linked asset failures by reason. Once those rules were added, the narrow run recovered 1649 of 1657 CSS-discovered assets and isolated the remaining eight failures to deterministic `http-404` cases instead of silent live leakage.
nextAction: Use `css-recovery-summary.json` and page-level CSS recovery status to decide whether remaining fidelity gaps come from missing first-paint assets or from stricter runtime/bootstrap dependencies.

title: Server-rendered portal shells should not treat every secondary async module as a strict blocker
summary: A fresh Jongno portal rerun showed that a same-site ticket endpoint could remain in the strict replay set even though the captured HTML already delivered the page title, navigation, landmarks, and dense visible shell. Generic replay diagnostics improve when secondary ticket, queue, calendar, reservation, and similar widget APIs are allowed to downgrade into supporting runtime traffic once strong server-rendered shell evidence exists.
nextAction: Keep using server-rendered shell evidence to distinguish secondary async module refreshes from true first-paint blockers on portal-style sites.

title: Portal replay debugging needs runtime error evidence separated from route and asset checks
summary: The latest Jongno rerun reached the correct local route and recovered most CSS assets, but replay still emitted a same-origin TypeError and multiple console errors from missing runtime resources. Stability work is more actionable when replay verification records console errors, uncaught exceptions, and failed runtime requests separately from route misses or blocked external requests, because that makes the remaining fidelity gap diagnosable without re-running the browser manually.
nextAction: Use runtime error evidence to decide whether the next generic fix should target hidden navigation, same-origin missing resources, or DOM assumptions inside legacy portal scripts.

title: Replay-only runtime guards should absorb same-origin DOM assumption failures without site-specific patches
summary: A portal replay can reach the correct local route, load most assets, and still degrade because same-origin scripts assume DOM nodes or resource files that are absent in the replayed page. A thin replay-only runtime guard improves stability when it records uncaught errors, wrapped callback failures, and same-origin resource load errors as reusable failure classes like `runtime-dom-assumption`, `runtime-script-failed`, and `runtime-resource-missing` instead of patching individual function names.
nextAction: Keep using replay-only guards to classify same-origin runtime degradation before considering any function-level or site-specific JavaScript patches.

title: Boilerplate-heavy pages need multi-profile content comparison instead of whole-body text drift alone
summary: A fresh Jongno public rerun showed that replay verification became more trustworthy once heading markers, main-content markers, and whole-body text were compared separately instead of letting `body.innerText.length` and flat token lists dominate the judgment. Generic fidelity reporting improves when verifier can mark `comparison-noise-likely`, attach title comparison confidence, and treat boilerplate-heavy navigation or footer text as a source of comparison noise rather than an automatic content failure.
nextAction: Keep using heading/main/body comparison profiles before escalating content drift on public portals, and only treat drift as high-confidence when main-content overlap is also weak.
